title: Software
text:
My GitHub page has the most up-to-date list of software projects and repositories. Below are selected research software projects developed by my students, collaborators, and me. Some are current research tools; others are historical packages associated with published work.



sdp: deep nonparametric estimation of discrete conditional distributions via smoothed dyadic partitioning. By Wesley Tansey.
gfl: a fast and flexible algorithm for solving the graph-fused lasso on an arbitrary graph with arbitrary smooth convex loss. By Wesley Tansey.
smoothfdr: methods exploiting spatial structure in multiple-testing problems on spatial lattices or arbitrary graphs. By Wesley Tansey.
FDRreg: tools for false-discovery-rate problems, including false-discovery-rate regression.
helloPG: an R package skeleton for incorporating the Pólya–Gamma distribution into your own code or package.
BayesLogit: Bayesian modeling of discrete outcomes via logistic and negative-binomial regression using Pólya–Gamma data augmentation. Developed by Jesse Windle.
BayesBridge: Bayesian bridge regression. Developed by Jesse Windle.

BayesLogit: Bayesian modeling of discrete outcomes via logistic and negative-binomial regression using Pólya–Gamma data augmentation. Developed by Jesse Windle.
BayesBridge: Bayesian bridge regression. Developed by Jesse Windle.

title: Books
text:
AIQ: How People and Machines Are Smarter Together was published in 2018, several years before the arrival of ChatGPT and the current wave of public attention to generative AI. We did not get everything right, but I am proud of how well the book has held up. AIQ tried to explain artificial intelligence as a practical, statistically driven technology rather than as science fiction, and it anticipated many questions that now feel urgent: What happens when chatbots become fluent enough to seem human? How should we think about algorithmic decision-making in hiring, finance, medicine, and public policy? What happens when both sides of a system, such as HR managers screening résumés and applicants writing them, begin using AI against each other?
The book remains an accessible account of how data, prediction, and human judgment interact in the real world. For a non-technical version of how this technology actually works, and sometimes does not work, it remains relevant.





“There comes a time in the life of a subject when someone steps up and writes the book about it.”
— Steven D. Levitt, co-author of Freakonomics


“Nick Polson and James Scott take us under the hood of AI and data science, showing that behind most algorithms is the story of a person trying to solve a problem and make the world better.”
— Michael J. Casey, author of The Age of Cryptocurrency and The Truth Machine


“At last, a book on the ideas behind AI and data science by people who really understand data.”
— David Spiegelhalter, Professor of the Public Understanding of Risk, University of Cambridge




Data Science in R: A Gentle Introduction is my free textbook on data science, used in a number of introductory classes at UT and elsewhere. The book is structured as a series of walk-through lessons in R that have students doing real data science quickly. It covers both the core ideas of data science and the concrete software skills needed to translate those ideas into practice.
Many lessons operate on the premise of “mimic first, understand later.” I introduce bits of R code that do something interesting and ask students to mimic them word for word to see what they do, without necessarily understanding every detail at first. The theory is that seeing the “what” first creates motivation to understand the “how” and “why.”

title: Research
text:
Google Scholar has an up-to-date list of my papers.
My research focuses on Bayesian statistics, scalable computation, and interdisciplinary applications. I have worked on foundational problems in Bayesian inference, including shrinkage priors, sparse inference, and multiple testing, and I was a co-inventor of the horseshoe prior, now widely used in Bayesian statistics and machine learning. I also co-developed the Pólya–Gamma data augmentation framework for fully Bayesian logistic regression. My applied work spans real-time disease forecasting, maternal and child health, telemedicine access, and public health and policy. Current projects focus on the statistical reliability of machine-learning methods, especially generative models and neural posterior estimation.


My methodological work includes Bayesian shrinkage, sparse inference, multiple testing, high-dimensional regression, and scalable posterior computation. A recurring theme is the development of Bayesian methods that preserve uncertainty while remaining usable in realistic data-analysis settings.



Recent work studies the reliability of machine-learning methods, especially generative models, simulation-based inference, neural posterior estimation, and methods whose behavior depends on the fit between a statistical model and the real data-generating process.



I collaborate on projects in health care, infectious disease, maternal and child health, telemedicine access, policy evaluation, security, neuroscience, finance, management, astronomy, molecular biology, political science, and linguistics. These projects typically involve observational data, uncertainty quantification, causal or policy-relevant claims, and careful communication of empirical evidence.




Bayesian shrinkage priors and sparse inference
Multiple testing and false-discovery-rate methods
Pólya–Gamma data augmentation and Bayesian logistic regression
Statistical reliability of generative models and simulation-based inference
Public-health applications, including disease forecasting and telemedicine access
Data science in business, policy, and interdisciplinary research




I’ve had the privilege to work with some very smart, dedicated, and wonderful Ph.D. students over the years. Current advisees and alumni include:

Vansh Bansal, current Ph.D. student
Tianyu Chen, current Ph.D. student
Min Chen, current Ph.D. student
Mauricio Garcia-Tec, now at Amazon AI
Jennifer Starling, now at Mathematica
Spencer Woody, now at Amgen
Oscar Madrid Padilla, now at UCLA
Wesley Tansey, now at Memorial Sloan-Kettering
Liang Sun, now at Microsoft
Jesse Windle, now at Hi-Fidelity Genetics

title: Consulting and Expert Work
text:
I provide consulting and expert testimony on statistical, data science, machine-learning, and AI-related issues in litigation, regulatory, business, and policy settings. My work focuses on helping attorneys, companies, courts, and decision-makers understand what can and cannot be concluded from data, models, algorithms, experiments, observational studies, and statistical analyses.



Many legal and regulatory disputes turn on empirical evidence: surveys, statistical models, machine-learning systems, observational studies, causal claims, damages analyses, anomaly detection methods, or large-scale data pipelines. In these settings, the central questions are often about the reliability of data, the interpretation of statistical conclusions, and the fit between the analysis and the legal or business issue at hand.
I have served as a consulting and testifying expert in a variety of such matters. My goal is to produce work that is technically rigorous, legally useful, and clear to non-specialists. In prior engagements, counsel have specifically praised the clarity, organization, rigor, and usefulness of my expert reports in matters involving complex statistical evidence.



I work on matters where data analysis or statistical reasoning is central to the dispute. This often includes questions about whether an empirical analysis is reliable, whether it supports the conclusion being offered, and whether its assumptions, data, and methods are appropriate for the legal or business question.
My work commonly involves statistical evidence, causal claims, machine-learning and AI systems, sampling and surveys, data quality, anomaly detection, and large-scale analyses of operational data. Across these areas, the recurring issues are similar: what the data show, what they do not show, how much uncertainty remains, and whether the analysis has been interpreted fairly.



I can assist at different stages of a matter, including early case assessment, discovery planning, analysis of produced data, expert reports, deposition preparation, rebuttal analysis, and testimony.
Typical engagements may involve:

Evaluating whether a statistical or machine-learning analysis supports the claim being made
Reviewing expert reports, code, data pipelines, model assumptions, and methodological choices
Identifying flaws in sampling, study design, causal reasoning, or statistical interpretation
Designing or critiquing empirical studies
Developing affirmative statistical analyses where the evidence supports them
Translating technical issues into language suitable for attorneys, courts, regulators, arbitrators, or business audiences
Preparing for deposition, cross-examination, Daubert issues, or expert rebuttal




Statistical expertise is often useful when a matter turns on whether empirical evidence is reliable, relevant, and strong enough to support the conclusions being offered. Recurring questions include:

What are the biggest statistical vulnerabilities in the opposing expert’s report?
Does the analysis support the expert’s conclusion, or does the opinion go beyond what the data can show?
Is the methodology consistent with accepted statistical practice and with how the method is actually used in the relevant field?
Does the evidence support a causal claim, or only an association?

Could an apparent pattern be explained by chance, data-processing choices, data leakage, or analytical discretion?

If the data came from a sample, was the sample conducted and interpreted appropriately?

Are the assumptions behind the model appropriate for the legal issue, population, time period, or decision being analyzed?
Are reported error rates, confidence intervals, p-values, probabilities, or uncertainty estimates being interpreted correctly?
Were the data filtered, linked, weighted, coded, or transformed in a way that could bias the result?
Has the analysis accounted for confounding, selection effects, missing data, measurement error, multiple comparisons, or model instability?
Does a machine-learning or AI system perform reliably for the relevant population and use case?
Are damages, exposure, risk, prevalence, discrimination, liability, or treatment-effect estimates supported by the available data?
Can the proposed analysis answer the question on a class-wide, population-wide, or case-specific basis?





Professor of Statistics, University of Texas at Austin, with faculty appointments in the Department of Statistics and Data Sciences and the McCombs School of Business.
Recipient of the Regents’ Outstanding Teaching Award, the highest teaching honor in the University of Texas System

Author or coauthor of more than 100 published scientific articles, with a research record spanning statistics, machine learning/AI, computational statistics, and interdiscplinary scientific collaborations.
Coauthor of AIQ: How People and Machines Are Smarter Together, described by the Wall Street Journal as “a book about AI with a moral core”
Author of Data Science in R: A Gentle Introduction, a free online textbook used in introductory statistics and data science courses at UT Austin and elsewhere




Email: james.scott@austin.utexas.edu
For legal matters, please include the names of the parties, counsel, and a brief non-confidential description of the matter so that I can conduct a conflicts check. Please do not send confidential material until an engagement is in place.

title: James Scott
text:
Professor of Statistics and Data Sciences · University of Texas at Austin



I am a statistician and data scientist at UT Austin, where I serve as Chair of the Department of Statistics and Data Sciences. My work spans Bayesian statistics, statistical machine learning, scalable computation, and applied data science in public health, medicine, policy, and business.

Research Books Teaching Consulting CV

title: James Scott
text:
Research
My research focuses on Bayesian inference, statistical machine learning, statistical computing, and interdisciplinary applications. I have worked on shrinkage priors, sparse inference, multiple testing, Bayesian computation, and the statistical reliability of machine-learning methods.
Research →

title: James Scott
text:
Books
I am coauthor of AIQ: How People and Machines Are Smarter Together and author of Data Science in R: A Gentle Introduction, a free textbook used in introductory data-science courses at UT and elsewhere.
Books →

title: James Scott
text:
Teaching
I teach statistical modeling, data science, probability, inference, and machine learning to students in statistics, data science, business, economics, and related fields.
Teaching →

title: James Scott
text:
Consulting and expert work
I consult on statistical, data-scientific, and machine-learning questions in litigation and other high-stakes settings, with particular attention to uncertainty, causal claims, sampling, model validity, and empirical evidence.
Consulting →
